title: Six-pointed snowflake papercut
subtitle: Folding and cutting a symmetrical six-pointed snowflake from a single square of paper
excerpt: A six-pointed snowflake is cut from a single square of paper folded into a 60-degree wedge. Cuts made in the wedge repeat six times across the full circle when opened. The mathematics of the fold guarantee six-fold symmetry; the design itself is determined by how and where you cut into the wedge edges.
sourceNotes: Paper snowflake cutting is a widely practised folk craft tradition with origins in European and East Asian paper-folding traditions. The geometric folding method described here is a synthesis of standard six-fold symmetry folding sequences documented in multiple paper craft manuals.

content.text: design requires folding the paper into a

that represents one-sixth of the full circle. Every cut made in the wedge appears six times in the opened design. The fold sequence has six steps and must be precise: even a few degrees of error in the 60-degree fold angle will produce an asymmetric result that does not close cleanly. The design possibilities within the wedge range from simple V-notches to complex lace-like openings.

items.name: Thin paper, 70 to 90 gsm, cut into a square (20 cm x 20 cm for a first attempt)
Small pointed scissors (best for cutting the folded wedge)
Pencil (for marking cuts on the wedge before cutting)

content.text: Fold the square in half to a rectangle, crease firmly.
Fold the rectangle in half again to a smaller square, then open back to a rectangle. This crease marks the centre of the long edge.
Fold the bottom-left corner up to the centre crease mark on the top edge at a 60-degree angle. The left edge of the paper now runs at 60 degrees from horizontal. Crease.
Fold the bottom-right section behind to align with the fold made in step 3. Both layers should now stack on top of each other. Crease.
Fold the whole assembly in half along the vertical centre line of the wedge. You now have a tall, narrow triangular wedge of 12 paper layers.
Trim the top of the wedge flat (the two angled edges should form a straight line across the top). This trimmed top becomes the outer edge of the snowflake.

Sketch a simple design in pencil on the folded wedge. V-notches cut into the sides produce spike patterns when opened; semicircles cut out of the sides produce oval openings. A pointed notch cut from the tip produces a hexagonal centre hole.
Cut carefully through all layers. Leave at least 3 mm of paper between any two cuts or between a cut and the edge.
Open the snowflake slowly, unfolding one layer at a time to avoid tearing. Press flat under a heavy book for a few minutes if the paper creases badly on opening.
